sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Ticks are parasites that feed on animal and human blood. There are two families of ticks found in Australia: hard ticks and soft ticks. The most common hard ticks encountered in Western Australia (WA) are the ornate kangaroo ticks and the brown dog tick. Soft ticks more commonly parasitise birds, marsupials and horses, rather than people.
Peak tick season in WA runs from September to April.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
Tick Bite Reactions
- Localised reactions, redness, itchiness
- Anaphylaxis can occur but this is not common
- Small itchy, bruise like mark after removal Rickettsial Disease
- Fever
- Rash
- Myalgia
- Headache
- Fatigue
- Eschar (dead tissue at the tick bite site)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Disturbing a tick can trigger further allergen release and can precipitate anaphylaxis, including in people without a prior history of tick allergy
- Do not apply methylated spirits, kerosene, petroleum jelly, or other irritants to the tick
- If there are symptoms suggestive of rickettsial infection such as fever, headache, rash, or eschar, arrange medical review
- Tick paralysis is uncommon but should be considered if progressive weakness, ataxia, or cranial nerve symptoms develop

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Keep patient at rest and observe closely for anaphylaxis
- Conduct Vital Sign Survey
- If signs of anaphylaxis develop, treat immediately as per anaphylaxis guideline
- For small ticks (larvae or nymphs), apply permethrin cream if available
- For adult ticks, freeze the tick in place with an ether-containing freezing spray if available and allow it to drop off
- Avoid freezing sprays and permethrin near the eyes, genitals, or ear canal; seek medical assistance for these sites
- If the patient has known tick anaphylaxis, or if safe in-situ killing/removal is not possible, arrange urgent medical assessment rather than forcibly removing the tick
- If remote and professional help is unavailable, and there is no history of tick anaphylaxis, careful removal with very fine forceps close to the skin may be a last resort

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider ECG
- Consider IV access
- Consider alternative pain relief if cold compress ineffective
- Manage anaphylaxis as per CPG

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- There is no accepted evidence that Australian tick bites cause Lyme disease acquired in Australia
- Advise review if fever, rash, eschar, progressive weakness, or allergic symptoms develop after the bite
